### **Amazon Inventory Forecasting Examples** {#amazon-inventory-forecasting-examples-1 .unnumbered}

Below are a few examples of different use case scenarios so you can get
an idea of how different product situations are forecasted.

We will talk a lot about sales patterns and velocities in this section
but be sure to read on further down to truly understand how velocities
should be calculated as improperly calculating velocities can lead to
over-ordering or stock outs faster than anything else.

### **New Products** {#new-products .unnumbered}

New products present their own challenge in that you do not have current
data to forecast with. Most forecasting is based on past data so the
challenge in forecasting new products is that there is none or very
little.

Fortunately, we live in the Amazon age and there are many tools
available to help us to determine the sales velocity of a particular
product. Looking at the estimated monthly sales volume of other similar
products can help us to determine what we might expect to sell. Then, we
should determine what percentage of those sales we think we will be able
to take.

Once that baseline of a steady sales velocity is determined, we then
need to decide how aggressively we would like to launch. Some Amazon
pros set the rule of thumb for lanches at 50% of the average sales for
that product. For example, if the top-selling product is doing 4,500
units per month, 50% would be 2250 units. Divided by 30 days, that is 75
units per day. So your launch should push for 75 units per day. If you
do a 7-day launch, that would be 525 units. From that point, you would
add your steady sales volume as calculated above to ensure you have
enough inventory for the successful launch and maintenance of your new
product sales.

For this reason, in order to forecast new products, it is important to
have a manual velocity to override any other sales data that may be
computed.

![Calculate Average Velocity](media/image7.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.6666666666666665in"}

This way you can disregard the sales made during your launch and build
in a more steady sales pattern, then simply layer the launch units on
top of that.

![Inventory Timeline](media/image5.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.5694444444444446in"}

### **Steady, Established Products** {#steady-established-products .unnumbered}

When selling products that have steady, established sales that do not
fluctuate from season to season, looking at a longer term velocity such
as 30, 60, or 90 days tends to be a good rule of thumb. Now if you have
products that are fairly steady for most of the year but then tend to
sell well, say, during Christmas or Summer, **it may be that you
actually need two different approaches to your forecasting throughout
the year.**

During your steady sales periods, you'd want to opt for the 30, 60, or
90 days as mentioned above, or a combination of those such as an average
30, 60, and 90-day velocities.

![Calculate Average Velocity](media/image23.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.9583333333333335in"}

Then, **when it comes time to plan for your holiday or Summer sales
season, simply switch to using Last Year's Sales data**.

![Calculate Average Velocity Last year
sales](media/image20.jpg){width="6.5in" height="4.041666666666667in"}

You can even consider adding growth trends on top of last year's data to
further improve the accuracy of your projections. A favorite of many
sellers is using the Year-to-Date growth factor layered on top of Last
Year's Sales.

![](media/image16.jpg){width="6.5in" height="3.6666666666666665in"}

Then, of course, after your sales season is past and you start coming
into your normal sales again, you can switch back to your 30-day sales,
making sure that none of the heightened sales data is captured within
that 30 days. Some sellers even opt for the 15 or 7-day sales period
until they have a full 30 days of normal, non-seasonal sales.

### **Products Where You're Sharing the Buy Box** {#products-where-youre-sharing-the-buy-box .unnumbered}

When sharing the Buy Box, as with selling using the wholesale model,
sales tend to be less steady and tend to fluctuate much more frequently
than with a product that you consistently own the Buy Box on such as a
Private Label product. For this reason, using a more short term sales
velocity period tends to make more sense. **Many sellers using this
business model tend to use the 30, 15, 7, and even 2-day sales
calculations** and often a combination of these.

![Use Velocity Calculation](media/image35.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.986111111111111in"}

This is to allow for the fluctuation of the market as, generally, there
is much more fluctuation and there is also more flexibility in wholesale
restocking, as the **lead times tend to be much shorter at 1-2 weeks**.
This allows for faster response times in restocking and **so looking at
the more narrow time frames tends to improve accuracy on a shorter
restock cycle**.

### **Seasonal Products** {#seasonal-products .unnumbered}

As with steady, established products, seasonal products can utilize Last
Year's Sales data or a combination of Last Year's Sales and Year-to-Date
Trend data.

![Sales-Data-Wholesale-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image10.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.4444444444444446in"}

**But what happens when you have no data from last year?** In those
instances, **simply use this year's data and add a seasonal increase or
decrease as needed**. For example, if sales in your category are
expected to double during the holiday season, you can add a 100%
increase starting on Black Friday and extending through to December 23rd

![Seasonal-Spikes-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image1.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.0833333333333335in"}![Holiday-Season-Sales-Data-Wholesale-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image43.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="5.583333333333333in"}

You can **also add sales spikes for things like Black Friday, Cyber
Monday,** and any Lightning Deals you may have.

![Lightning-Deals-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image11.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.3055555555555554in"}

![Daily-Velocity-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image32.jpg){width="6.25in"
height="4.541666666666667in"}

### **Steady Growth** {#steady-growth .unnumbered}

For a product with steady sales and steady growth using Trends such as
Year-to-Date or 90-day trends can help you to forecast potential sales
demand.

![Calculate-Trend-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image16.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.6666666666666665in"}

Applying these trends will help you to forecast potential continued
sales growth.

### **Accelerated Growth** {#accelerated-growth .unnumbered}

For accelerated growth, using more short term trends, such as 15 or 30
days can serve you well.

![Accelerated-Growth-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image38.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.625in"}

This can sometimes be the best option for a product that is newly
launched and gaining ranking rapidly before settling out into a steady,
established product.

### **Planned Growth** {#planned-growth .unnumbered}

Products with planned growth but without the trend data to support their
growth can use a simple Additional Sales Growth increase. This would add
a percentage to all sales on top of whatever velocity is being used.

![Planned-Growth-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image51.jpg){width="6.25in"
height="3.013888888888889in"}

This can be the best option for products you are beginning aggressive
PPC campaigns for.

## **The Importance of Inventory Forecasting For Amazon Sellers** {#the-importance-of-inventory-forecasting-for-amazon-sellers .unnumbered}

![Reasons Why Sellers Should Invest In Inventory
Forecasting](media/image8.png){width="6.5in"
height="3.4166666666666665in"}

While it can sometimes seem obvious, it is valid to discuss why proper
forecasting is important as there are other subtle or less obvious
reasons for proper forecasting that are not often considered.

### **The Benefits of Accurate Inventory Forecasting** {#the-benefits-of-accurate-inventory-forecasting .unnumbered}

Inventory forecasting is important because it helps you to control your
cash flow and **improve your bottom line profitability by limiting stock
outs, over-ordering, and expensive air shipments**.

It is also becoming **increasingly more important to the Amazon
algorithm** as Amazon continues to push its own policies toward
inventory efficiency. The better you are at inventory management within
Amazon, the happier Amazon is.

### **Minimize Stock Outs** {#minimize-stock-outs .unnumbered}

Minimizing stock outs is important for a few reasons. **First is the
obvious, the revenue lost when stocking out has a direct impact on your
bottom line. The second is the potential loss of page rank and
subsequent cost of re-ranking your product**. An additional impact comes
in the negative effect on your Inventory Performance Index (IPI) score.
**Amazon's algorithm does not like to see stock outs and can thus
penalize your account by lowering your restock limits after a stock
out,** Something which could cause potential stock out cycles due to
decreasing restock limits.

Staying in stock helps you to avoid all of this and that helps you to
increase your bottom line.

### **Reduce Product Waste** {#reduce-product-waste .unnumbered}

Product waste, or inventory waste, is the waste within your business due
to inventory that does not quickly turn over, or sell. **This waste
would include the wasted money spent on storage fees, the wasted
opportunity due to tied up capital that could be utilized elsewhere**,
and any other waste that could occur due to the over-ordering of those
products.

When you forecast properly you avoid all of this inventory waste. Less
inventory on hand means less waste. The right inventory on hand means
minimizing waste and stock outs while maximizing profit.

### **Reduce Inventory Holding Costs** {#reduce-inventory-holding-costs .unnumbered}

Holding cost is incorporated into product waste but bears repeating.
Proper forecasting and ordering means that your inventory efficiency
improves bringing your inventory holding costs, i.e. storage fees, down.
**If you have to pay for 1 month of storage vs 3 months of storage, the
profit per unit will be higher**.

To sum it up, accurate ordering means more efficiency and less storage
cost and, therefore, higher profits for you.

### **Less Guess Stress** {#less-guess-stress .unnumbered}

Guess stress is real. **Amazon sellers often put off reordering due to
the added stress, frustration, and time. This procrastination can end up
leading to ordering too late**. Not knowing exactly what to do when
placing your next order can add extra time, doubt, and confusion to your
process.

**When you have an organized and automated system, it tends to eliminate
the guess stress** and to take a lot less time; therefore, you order
when you should, avoid stock outs, and keep your rankings and profits.

### **Maintain Ranking** {#maintain-ranking .unnumbered}

When your product runs out of stock, it can start to lose page ranking
rapidly. For that reason, keeping in stock becomes important beyond just
the lost revenue. The cost of re-ranking a product added on top of the
lost revenue can make stocking out a very costly mistake.

### **Increase Business Valuation** {#increase-business-valuation .unnumbered}

The better you manage your inventory, the less you stock out, the lower
your storage fees, and the better your profitability. At the end of the
day, profit is what investors look at when buying businesses. They
attend to give a multiple based on the overall profit of the business.
**This means that your profit will help to determine the overall value
of your business when it comes time to sell.** Businesses tend to sell
at a multiple of that profit. The more profit you keep in your business,
the higher that valuation if you ever decide to exit your business.

### **Protect Your IPI Score** {#protect-your-ipi-score .unnumbered}

Amazon has been taking inventory management much more seriously these
days. So seriously, in fact, that they've been essentially penalizing
sellers who have trouble keeping their products in stock. They have been
setting the amount of inventory you are able to send into Amazon FBA
warehouses based on your total sales for a given period. **This means
that if you stock out, you will be selling fewer total units over that
time period, and thus Amazon will not allow you to send as much
inventory the next time around**. This ties back into the need to
minimize stock outs we discussed above. While staying in stock is just
one factor affecting your IPI score, it can be an important one.

**Pro Tip:** Use an amazon forecasting software like
[SoStocked](https://www.sostocked.com/) to accurately forecast orders
and factor in things like past sales, future marketing, and seasonality.

## **Restock Limits, the Supply Chain and Inventory Forecasting** {#restock-limits-the-supply-chain-and-inventory-forecasting .unnumbered}

![Restock Limits Supply Chain And
Forecasting](media/image49.png){width="6.5in"
height="3.4166666666666665in"}

Other more recent factors that make accurately forecasting your
inventory demand more important than ever are restock limits and supply
chain constraints introduced with the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic.
Two things which are proving to have long-lasting effects.

With the introduction of universal [restock limit
restrictions](https://www.sostocked.com/amazon-restock-limits/) for all
sellers across the Amazon FBA network in 2020, inventory forecasting has
become more complex than ever, making good inventory management all the
more crucial.

Many sellers have found themselves forced into storing excess inventory
within third party warehouses and have had to send inventory into FBA
from these warehouses much more frequently.

In the past, **[inventory
turnover](https://www.sostocked.com/amazon-inventory-turnover/), one of
the key yardsticks in determining whether a business was doing a good
job with managing inventory,** was not a major concern for Amazon
sellers because they could generally send in as much inventory as they
wanted to without issue.

The post-Covid shifting of Amazon's inventory policies have forced
sellers to become better at managing inventory, a skillset most sellers
had not, to that point, had to hone.

Therefore, **inventory management has become much more important than
before.**

Add to that the increasing lead times and unpredictability of the supply
chain and techniques like incorporating a [buffer
stock](https://www.sostocked.com/buffer-stock/) into your process become
more crucial than ever. It's sort of like having a rainy day fund for
when your car breaks down. Supply chain emergencies are much better
weathered when you have an extra stash of inventory to float you until
your inventory finally arrives.

**The habits we lay out below are essential to any inventory-based
business at any time, but now more than ever** it is becoming clear why
running your business on sound inventory principles and practices is
vital to the health and stability of your business.

## **What is Amazon Inventory Forecasting?** {#what-is-amazon-inventory-forecasting .unnumbered}

![Inventory Forecasting Definition](media/image50.png){width="6.5in"
height="3.4166666666666665in"}

Amazon Inventory Forecasting is the action of determining what inventory
to order or reorder. Below are some methods and things to think about
when forecasting your Amazon inventory.

### **Are You Selling Goods As Fast As They Are Stocked?** {#are-you-selling-goods-as-fast-as-they-are-stocked .unnumbered}

This is what is known as sell-through or turn over. You want to increase
the rate of sell-through. **You do not want to take 5 or 6 months to
sell through an item as this means 5 or 6 months worth of storage fees
and 5 or 6 months of capital that is tied up in inventory that is not
moving.**

To optimize this sell-through rate, you want to determine how best to
avoid storage fees and unturned capital and limit these things as much
as possible. For example, if you are charged for storage each month, you
should potentially have a goal to turn over your inventory monthly or as
quickly as possible.

### **Inventory Management vs. Inventory Forecasting** {#inventory-management-vs.-inventory-forecasting .unnumbered}

While inventory management and forecasting tend to go hand-in-hand, they
are not the same thing. **Forecasting is the act or process of
determining how much to order to maintain optimal levels of inventory.**

**Inventory Management is the full function of ordering, storing and
selling through your inventory.** Included in this could be the
management of raw materials, sub-SKUS, or components for
kitting/bundling, and the finished, sellable product. Warehousing,
kitting, and prep are also part of your inventory management system.

### **What is Demand Forecasting?** {#what-is-demand-forecasting .unnumbered}

Demand forecasting is the method of trying to predict customer demand
for your products in order to calculate proper order quantities and
timing. While past sales may have some bearing on demand, it is not
based on actual orders or sales but on potential future sales.

### **Sales Forecasting vs. Demand Forecasting** {#sales-forecasting-vs.-demand-forecasting .unnumbered}

The main difference between sales and demand is that sales are actual
past or current orders made. Current sales could be in the form of
pre-orders that have not yet been fulfilled or wholesale orders
requested in the form of invoices that need to be filled.

All that said, **there are two types of forecasting which should be
considered as part of your overall forecasting process.**

Demand, as opposed to sale, is what customers want. Demand can change
overtime. Further, demand could be outwardly affected, whereas sales can
potentially be affected internally.

**One of the things that could outwardly affect demand would be where
your product is in its life cycle.** For example, just because your
product was all the rage and trending last year, does not mean it will
perform the same this year.

**Economic conditions can also impact demand.** If you have a premium
product and the economy goes through a recession, customers may tighten
their belts and stop purchasing your top line products, opting instead
for cheaper versions.

These are the types of things that could affect demand and should be
considered when doing demand forecasting.

When it comes to Sales Forecasting however, historical data, in part,
guides your forecasting. The other elements, however, that should factor
into your calculations would be things that you can help to control
internally.

Some of these things are sales, promotions, coupons or sales offers you
plan to run. **Essentially, sales forecasting would include past sales
data to help to predict future potential sales, plus the marketing
events you have planned** that could increase the sales above this
point.

### **What is Inventory Planning?** {#what-is-inventory-planning .unnumbered}

Inventory planning is the process of determining optimal levels of stock
and the best timing for placing orders.

In order to do proper inventory planning, **you should consider weaving
demand forecasting and sales forecasting seamlessly together** to help
you to accurately predict based on past sales, potential changes in
demand, and future planned marketing events.

This will help you to determine a reordering schedule that includes
ordering deadlines and quantities. It is wise to do 3, 6, or even 12
months of inventory planning in advance. Of course, nothing would be set
in stone until you actually place your orders as velocities and plans
fluctuate over the year but it would give you a good estimate of
inventory needs and cash flow. It is also very helpful to provide your
suppliers with this estimated reordering schedule so they, too, can do
their own inventory planning for raw materials and labor needed to
fulfill your orders.

## **How Does Amazon Inventory Forecasting Work?** {#how-does-amazon-inventory-forecasting-work .unnumbered}

![How Amazon Inventory Forecasting
Works](media/image25.png){width="6.5in" height="3.4166666666666665in"}

Learning sound inventory forecasting techniques helps you to build the
foundation for a system that will help you to place orders that are
right for your business and to off-load tasks to your VAs that you don't
want to do or don't have time for.

### **How To Fix Inventory Problems** {#how-to-fix-inventory-problems .unnumbered}

The three main inventory problems tend to be over-ordering (which
produces product waste), under-ordering (leading to stock outs), and
ordering too late, which also can cause stock outs.

The three most important "fixes" to these top inventory problems are:

**1. Removing Sales Spikes**

Sales spikes in your sales history that will not be guaranteed to repeat
themselves in the future need to be factored out of your daily sales
velocity. So, for example, if you had a Lightning Deal last year that
you will not have this year, you need to be able to remove that. *Not*
removing these things can be a prime contributor to over-ordering.

![Sales-Spikes-and-Lightning-Deal-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image31.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="4.305555555555555in"}

**2. Removing Stock Outs.**

Not eliminating stock outs from your forecast calculation can result in
the opposite problem. If you stocked out 10 out of the last 30 days but
are just using total sales to determine the next 30 days of stock, you
will end up ordering the same amount that caused you to stock out,
triggering a potential stock out cycle. You need to determine what your
average daily sales would have been without the stock outs so you can be
sure to order the correct amount of stock for next time.

![Stock-Outs-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image33.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="4.569444444444445in"}

**Factoring out things like sales spikes and stock outs is what we call
Adjusted Velocity.** You are adjusting the numbers to remove any of the
outliers that do not help you to order the correct amount of stock the
next time around. Adjusting these numbers gives you a better idea of
what should realistically happen in the future if all goes to plan.

**3. Adopting a Buffer Stock.**

Buffer stock, also called safety stock, is a little bit of extra stock
that you keep on hand as a cushion in case anything goes wrong with your
order and it arrives late. It is like the Empty light on your car when
running low on gas that warns you to fill up before you run out so you
have enough time to make it to the station.

![Adopting-Buffer-Stock-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image22.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.3472222222222223in"}

**The best way to calculate buffer stock is to use a multiple of days,
such as 30 days** of extra stock on hand so that your order arrives by
the time you hit 30 days left of stock, not zero days of stock.

### **How to Calculate Inventory** {#how-to-calculate-inventory .unnumbered}

To properly calculate current inventory, you must factor in several
things to ensure all of your current stock is accounted for.

**To start, you must get the proper inventory count at Amazon.** This is
a combination of (1) Available Inventory, (2) Reserved Inventory minus
Customer Orders, this is all inventory that is being processed within or
transferred between fulfillment centers, and (3) and Inbound FBA
Shipments that have not yet been checked into Amazon.

![FBA-Inventory-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image45.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.861111111111111in"}![Seasonal-Products-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image21.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.125in"}

This will give you an accurate account of inventory connected to Amazon.

**However, there are two other potential factors to account for: open
Purchase Orders and 3rd Party Warehouse Inventory.**

You must also account for any Purchase Orders that are in production or
en route to either your warehouse or Amazon. NOTE: Be careful not to
double count any Amazon Inbound Shipment inventory that is connected to
a pending Purchase Order.

All of this should be tracked by potential arrival dates to ensure there
is no gap in available inventory.

![3rd-Party-Warehouse-Inventory-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image30.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="4.333333333333333in"}

Then, any inventory you are currently storing at 3rd Party Warehouses
should be accounted for as well.

![Inventory-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image4.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="1.0694444444444444in"}

With all of these elements in place, you should have the starting point
to begin forecasting your inventory orders.

### **How to Forecast Inventory** {#how-to-forecast-inventory .unnumbered}

Once your inventory has been properly calculated and accounted for, you
need historical sales data along with supplier lead times and order
frequency or an order schedule as the main elements for forecasting.

Historical sales data can be pulled from your Amazon sales reports via
download or directly from an Amazon API connection like SoStocked does.

![Historical-Sales-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image3.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.2777777777777777in"}

Your supplier lead times can be gotten from your suppliers and freight
forwarders by asking how long your items will take to produce and to
ship. Currently, due to all of the delays and problems arising along the
logistics and supply chain network, asking your suppliers and freight
forwarding companies more frequently for updates on their lead times is
an important practice which will help you to forecast more accurate
[reorder points](https://www.sostocked.com/amazon-reorder-point/).

![Edit-Lead-Time-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image9.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.7916666666666665in"}

**Order frequency is often based on Minimum Order Quantity and optimal
shipment sizes such as full container loads.** It should also be made,
in part, by determining how quickly you can turn, or sell through, your
inventory. The faster your sell-through, often the lower your storage
fees.

The more sophisticated way of setting your order frequency is to use
what is known as Min/Max Restocking.

**Min/Max Restocking is a way of setting a minimum and a maximum range
that you will always stay within.**

![Order-Schedule-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image52.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="5.152777777777778in"}

For example, if your minimum is at 30 days, you will be prompted to
order or transfer so that your inventory arrives before you hit 30 days
worth of inventory. If your max is set to 90 days, you will be told to
replenish up to 90 days each time you restock. So you will replenish to
90 days worth of inventory, then sell through to 30 days and restock
back up to 90. Your lead times and transfer times should be baked into
the equation so that you are told to transfer or order in time. So if
you have a 30-day minimum, or buffer stock, and your transfer time is 10
days, you will be told to transfer 40 days before you run out of stock.

**You should also consider setting a minimum and a maximum for your
warehouse inventory just so that you keep an extra cushion available to
hand.**

Lastly, setting your transfer frequency if you are using a 3rd party
warehouse to store excess inventory will also help you to systemize and
automate this process. The same min/max techniques that are applied to
order frequency can be applied to transfer frequency, although the lead
time will, of course, change.

### **The Most Important Metrics in Inventory Planning** {#the-most-important-metrics-in-inventory-planning .unnumbered}

The most important metrics for inventory planning are:

-   **Total Inventory, which includes:**

    -   Amazon Available Inventory

    -   Amazon Reserved Inventory minus Customer Orders

    -   Inventory en route to Amazon not yet checked in.

    -   Inventory at your 3rd party warehouse(s)

    -   Inventory in production

    -   Inventory en route to warehouse(s)

    > **Adjusted Sales Velocity, which includes:**

    -   Daily Sales Velocity

    -   Adjusted for Sales Spikes

    -   Adjusted for Stock Outs

    -   Adjusted for Deep Discounts

    -   Trend (optional)

    > Order Schedule using a set frequency such as monthly or variable
    > frequency using Min/Max Restocking.

    > Transfer Schedule, same as above.

    > Seasonality, any predicted sales increases or decreases based on
    > historical patterns or demand forecasting.

    > **Marketing Events for sales forecasting such as:**

    -   Lightning Deals

    -   Email Campaigns

    -   Changes to PPC Campaigns

    -   Coupon Promotions

    -   Influencer Promotions

    -   Any other planned marketing campaigns

    > Cash flow or cash on hand, to ensure you can afford the orders
    > that need to be placed.

Knowing all of the above will help you to properly build out a sound
inventory management system to stay in stock, reduce product waste, and
avoid expensive, emergency air shipments.

## **How To Use Inventory Forecasting Software To Automate Your Inventory Processes** {#how-to-use-inventory-forecasting-software-to-automate-your-inventory-processes .unnumbered}

![Recommended Amazon Inventory Forecasting
Tools](media/image27.png){width="6.5in" height="3.4166666666666665in"}

Using an [Amazon inventory management
software](https://www.sostocked.com/) can be a game-changer as it can
handle, process, and sort data much better than simply using
spreadsheets and downloaded Amazon reports.

However, it is extremely important to know how the software works, what
algorithms it is using, and how it is computing your data. A software
that is too secretive about how it looks at your data or that uses the
wrong calculations may not be as helpful as you think and could actually
be more harmful than helpful. An inventory software should be able to do
all we discussed in this post. It should be fully transparent, fully
understandable and fully customizable.

It should allow you to remove sales spikes and stock outs and not just
look at inventory on hand but should treat listing suspensions as stock
outs as well, something most inventory software systems don't currently
do. It should be able to handle your orders and your transfers and
should allow you to add marketing events into your inventory planning.

All that said, **with the right inventory software, what benefits can
you expect?**

Setting up things such as buffer stock within a software will mean that
you can get notifications to place orders and transfers well before you
run out so you can avoid stock outs and expensive air freight.

You will have an accurate accounting of all of your inventory in one
place to track all shipments, inventory, and projections so that you are
always prepared.

[SoStocked](https://www.sostocked.com/) does all of these things and
more. It was designed by an Amazon seller and has gone through many
updates and improvements based on the feedback from hundreds of
successful, seasoned sellers.

SoStocked users have streamlined their processes, cut many hours out of
their weekly and monthly management, and saved money by avoiding stock
outs and air shipments.

Set up within SoStocked can be as quick as a few hours following our
in-app guides and tutorials. After your account has been set up, you can
receive daily or weekly emails when any orders or transfers are needed.
A great system, long term means automation, and SoStocked was designed
to help you with just that.

## **Best Practices for Inventory Forecasting** {#best-practices-for-inventory-forecasting .unnumbered}

![Calculate Your Amazon Inventory Forecasts With These
Tips](media/image29.png){width="6.5in" height="3.4166666666666665in"}

To wrap up, below are some of the additional best practices that we and
our users have adopted to truly ensure an inventory plan that works.

### **Sync with Marketing and Accounting** {#sync-with-marketing-and-accounting .unnumbered}

One of the primary reasons for inventory issues is marketing in a
vacuum. By this is meant that sellers often begin on a successful
marketing campaign but don't think to vet this campaign against their
inventory to see how inventory levels will be affected and whether they
can keep up with the marketing changes. A good marketing plan then can
turn bad real quick when it stocks out your best seller.

**Adding your marketing campaign projections into your inventory plans
helps you to see whether your plan will cause you to stock out.** Using
this data, you can adjust your marketing accordingly to ensure you stay
in stock and increase future orders to keep pace with growth.

![Lightning-Deals-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image11.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="3.3055555555555554in"}![Seasonal-Spikes-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image1.jpg){width="6.5in"
height="2.0833333333333335in"}

![Marketing-Campaign-Projections-Automate-Amazon-Inventory-Forecasting](media/image36.jpg){width="6.25in"
height="3.9444444444444446in"}

To take it one step further, checking with your accounting team to make
sure they will have the cash on hand to afford future inventory orders
helps to round out the entire sales cycle so that everyone is on the
same page and working together for the success of the company as a
whole.

### **What is "Good Data"?** {#what-is-good-data .unnumbered}

This is just a final plug for using Adjusted Daily Velocity. Using good
data rather than gut feel helps you to place orders appropriate to your
business. This means that you are not putting sales spikes and stock
outs into your data and repeating past mistakes such as over and
under-ordering.

You also need to ensure that you are keeping real-time stock levels so
that you don't double count or miss any inventory, which can also mess
up your order amounts.

### **Always Have a Backup Plan** {#always-have-a-backup-plan .unnumbered}

Lastly, always have a backup plan. **Don't rely on Amazon to be the
keeper of your business.** If they lose inventory or take forever
checking it in or restrict the number of units you can send, you may
find yourself facing an inventory nightmare that can really have a harsh
impact on your bottom line.

For that reason, making sure you have a [3rd party warehouse and
distribution
system](https://www.sostocked.com/3pl-logistics-backup-for-amazon/) set
up can help you to keep sales coming in even when Amazon can't seem to
properly stock your items into FBA.

**Like I always like to say, even if Amazon is your only sales channel,
it shouldn't be your only distribution channel.**

Inventory management can seem complicated, with lots of moving parts,
but **getting the foundation in place can mean you create a system that
works for you and can greatly improve your bottom-line profitability.**
After all, it's not what you make, it's what you keep.

## **What's Your Next Move?** {#whats-your-next-move .unnumbered}

![Use SoStocked To Automate Amazon Inventory
Forecasting](media/image17.png){width="6.5in"
height="3.4166666666666665in"}

Now that you have a deeper understanding of inventory management and
forecasting processes, it's time to start thinking about how to put
these plans into action in your own business.

I hope this primer was helpful and provided you with a lot of great
ideas that you can implement to make your business run more efficiently
and, above all, more profitably. Putting in more structure and order
will help you to stay in stock and to avoid the costly mistake of
over-ordering which, in turn, improves cash flow and increases your
margins overall.

**So where do you go from here?** Here are some things I suggest taking
on right away to most dramatically impact your processes and improve
your inventory health both immediately and long term.

1.  Start by focusing on what I feel are some of the most important best
    > practices for inventory management: daily adjusted velocity,
    > buffer stock, and min/max restocking.

2.  Automate more of your processes by signing up for an inventory
    > management and forecasting software like
    > [SoStocked.com](https://www.sostocked.com/).

3.  Bring your VAs and inventory management team on board by continuing
    > to systemize your reorder and transfer process. Our [Inventory
    > Management Training
    > Guide](https://www.sostocked.com/amazon-inventory-management-training/),
    > filled with helpful resources, can help to equip your team with
    > the tools and know-how needed to manage your inventory so you can
    > focus on marketing, sales and other revenue-generating aspects of
    > your business.

Here's a **practitioner-grade glossary** of the key **artifacts and
terms** you'll use in an Asia→US/Canada, Amazon-focused e-commerce
supply chain. I have grouped them so that your Supply Chain Manager (and
officers) can use this as a reference for operating purposes. Each item
includes what it is and why it matters; artifacts (the repeatable
documents/reports/dashboards) are explicitly called out.

# **Planning & Forecasting**

-   **S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)** --- A monthly
    > cross-functional process that reconciles demand, supply, and
    > financial targets. Aligns marketing pushes with capacity and
    > inventory to avoid stockouts/overstock.

-   **Demand Forecast** (artifact) --- Monthly baseline forecast by
    > SKU/market (US/CA), usually rolling 6--12 months. Drives PO
    > quantities and FBA/AWD/3PL split.

-   **MAPE / Forecast Accuracy** --- Mean Absolute Percentage Error;
    > measures forecast quality. Lower is better; used to set safety
    > stock and trust in plans.

-   **Forecast Bias** --- Systematic over-/under-forecasting. Positive =
    > over, negative = under. Guides correction factors.

-   **Seasonality / Trend** --- Pattern decomposition used to adjust
    > forecasts for holidays, Prime-type events, etc.

-   **Safety Stock** --- Buffer inventory to protect a target service
    > level given demand and lead-time variability.

-   **Reorder Point (ROP)** --- Inventory level at which you trigger
    > replenishment: lead-time demand + safety stock.

-   **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** --- Theoretical order size that
    > minimizes ordering + holding cost; sanity check for MOQ-driven
    > buys.

-   **ABC/XYZ Segmentation** --- Rank SKUs by value/velocity (ABC) and
    > demand volatility (XYZ) to tailor service levels and review
    > cadences.

# **Product & Master Data**

-   **SKU** --- Internal stock-keeping unit. Must map 1:1 to ASIN
    > listings and packaging variants.

-   **ASIN** --- Amazon's catalog identifier; one ASIN may have
    > variations (size/color).

-   **FNSKU** --- Amazon's scannable label to attribute inventory to
    > your seller account. Required on each sellable unit for FBA.

-   **UPC / GTIN** --- GS1 barcodes for retail identification. Must be
    > valid/owned to avoid listing issues.

-   **Case Pack / Inner Pack** --- Units per carton/inner; affects
    > cartonization, prep cost, and compliance.

-   **Carton Spec Sheet** (artifact) --- Dimensions/weight, units per
    > carton, labeling/markings. Prevents inbound non-compliance.

-   **BOM (Bill of Materials)** --- Components per finished good; useful
    > for kits/bundles (PPK).

-   **Golden Sample** --- Approved reference unit used by factory and QC
    > to compare production output.

# **Procurement & Supplier Management**

-   **RFQ (Request for Quote)** --- Structured inquiry to suppliers with
    > specs, volumes, Incoterms; bases cost negotiations.

-   **MOQ (Minimum Order Quantity)** --- Factory minimum per SKU or per
    > PO; drives buy-box economics.

-   **Purchase Requisition (PR)** (artifact) --- Internal request to
    > create a PO; control point for approvals.

-   **Purchase Order (PO)** (artifact) --- Binding order to supplier:
    > SKUs, qty, price, terms, Incoterms, delivery window.

-   **PO Acknowledgement** --- Supplier sign-back confirming price, lead
    > time, pack, and ship terms.

-   **Supplier Scorecard** (artifact) --- Monthly OTIF, quality defects,
    > responsiveness, pricing adherence; supports re-sourcing decisions.

-   **Payment Terms** --- e.g., 30% deposit / 70% against BL; or Net-X
    > days. Impacts cash flow and risk.

-   **Incoterms** --- Trade terms defining cost/risk handoff:

    -   **EXW** (Ex-Works): you pick up at factory; you own risk early.

    -   **FOB** (Free On Board): supplier clears export & loads, risk
        > transfers on vessel load.

    -   **FCA**: handoff to carrier at named place; common for air.

    -   **CIF/CIP**: supplier pays freight/insurance to destination;
        > risk still transfers earlier.

    -   **DAP**: delivered to place, duties unpaid.

    -   **DDP**: delivered duty paid; supplier handles import---control
        > often limited.

# **Quality Assurance**

-   **AQL (Acceptable Quality Limit)** --- Statistically sampled
    > inspection standard (e.g., 2.5 major/4.0 minor).

-   **Pre-Production Inspection (PPI)** --- Confirms materials/processes
    > before mass run.

-   **DUPRO (During Production)** --- Mid-run check to catch systemic
    > defects early.

-   **PSI (Pre-Shipment Inspection)** --- Final random inspection before
    > balance payment.

-   **QC Report** (artifact) --- Photo-rich pass/fail with defect
    > taxonomy; gate for shipment release.

-   **Corrective Action Plan (CAP)** (artifact) --- Root-cause,
    > corrections, and prevention for recurring defects.

# **Logistics & Transportation**

-   **Mode** --- **Air Express** (fastest, costliest), **Air Freight**,
    > **Ocean FCL** (full container), **Ocean LCL** (consolidated),
    > **Rail/Intermodal**, **Truck (LTL/FTL)**.

-   **Freight Forwarder** --- Orchestrates international transport,
    > consolidations, and documents.

-   **Booking Confirmation** (artifact) --- Space secured on
    > flight/vessel; includes ETD/ETA and cut-offs.

-   **Bill of Lading (BL)** --- Ocean transport document & title;
    > **MBL** (Master, carrier) vs **HBL** (House, forwarder).

-   **Air Waybill (AWB/MAWB/HAWB)** --- Air transport document.

-   **VGM** --- Verified Gross Mass required for ocean shipments.

-   **Drayage** --- Port to warehouse trucking; sensitive to
    > demurrage/detention.

-   **Demurrage / Detention** --- Fees for late pickup/late container
    > return; avoided via tight appointment mgmt.

-   **Freight Class / NMFC** --- US LTL freight classification; affects
    > rates.

# **Customs & Trade Compliance (US/Canada)**

-   **HS/HTS Code** --- Classification driving duty rates and
    > admissibility; misclassification is costly.

-   **Customs Broker** --- Files entries, calculates duty/taxes, manages
    > compliance.

-   **ISF (10+2)** --- US ocean import pre-advice; must file before
    > vessel lading to avoid penalties.

-   **COO (Certificate of Origin)** --- Proves origin for duties/FTAs
    > (e.g., USMCA).

-   **PGA Requirements** --- Agencies like FDA, FCC, EPA, CFIA; may
    > require permits/tests.

-   **MSDS/SDS** --- Safety data for hazardous materials; needed for
    > air/ocean carriage and 3PL.

-   **Continuous Bond / Single-Entry Bond** --- Financial guarantee for
    > US imports.

-   **IOR/NRI** --- Importer of Record (US) / Non-Resident Importer
    > (Canada); defines who is legally responsible.

# **Warehousing: 3PL, AWD, FBA**

-   **3PL (Third-Party Logistics)** --- External warehouse for storage,
    > prep, and omnichannel fulfillment.

-   **AWD (Amazon Warehousing & Distribution)** --- Bulk inbound to
    > Amazon's upstream network with onward transfer to FBA; used to
    > reduce FBA storage strain.

-   **FBA (Fulfillment by Amazon)** --- Amazon stores, picks, packs,
    > ships, and handles CS/returns; fees per unit and storage.

-   **FC (Fulfillment Center)** --- Amazon's warehouse destination code
    > (e.g., LGB8).

-   **ASN (Advance Shipment Notice)** --- Heads-up to 3PL/AWD/FBA on
    > inbound contents; enables receiving appointments.

-   **Inbound Shipment Plan** (artifact) --- Amazon-generated routing:
    > **SPD** (small parcel/UPS), **LTL**, **FTL**; includes **Shipment
    > ID**.

-   **Partnered Carrier Program** --- Discounted SPD/LTL to FCs via
    > Amazon partners.

-   **SCAC / PRO** --- Carrier ID and tracking reference for LTL/FTL.

-   **Palletization Standard** --- Typically 40x48", height/weight
    > limits, corner boards, wrap; prevents rejections.

-   **AWD Transfer Order** (artifact) --- Instruction to move stock from
    > AWD to specific FBA FCs.

# **Amazon Operations & Inventory Health**

-   **IPI (Inventory Performance Index)** --- Amazon's score for
    > inventory efficiency (excess, sell-through, stranded); influences
    > storage limits.

-   **Aged/Overaged Inventory** --- Inventory held beyond Amazon
    > thresholds; incurs surcharges. Managed via **Overaged Inventory
    > Analysis** (artifact) and actions (markdowns, removals, AWD
    > buffering).

-   **Stranded Inventory** --- On-hand but not sellable due to
    > listing/linking issues; requires relinking fixes.

-   **Reserved / Receiving / Available** --- Amazon status buckets:
    > reserved (transfers/FC moves), receiving (inbound not yet
    > available), available (sellable).

-   **Removal Order / Disposal Order** (artifact) --- Command to pull or
    > scrap FBA stock to cut fees.

-   **BSR (Best Seller Rank)** --- Relative demand proxy; helps classify
    > velocity tiers.

# **Inventory Management & Analytics**

-   **On-Hand / On-Order / In-Transit** --- Inventory positions at
    > 3PL/AWD/FBA, at supplier, and on the water/air.

-   **ATP (Available to Promise)** --- Net available inventory after
    > allocations/reservations.

-   **Days of Supply / Weeks of Cover** --- On-hand ÷ average
    > daily/weekly sales; primary stock health metric.

-   **Inventory Turnover** --- COGS ÷ average inventory; higher
    > indicates capital efficiency.

-   **Fill Rate** --- % of demand served from stock; customer service
    > lens.

-   **Cycle Count** --- Periodic counting to reconcile WMS vs physical.

# **Finance, Costing & Accounting**

-   **COGS (Cost of Goods Sold)** --- Product cost recognized when sold;
    > includes landed cost where policy applies.

-   **Landed Cost** --- COGS + freight + duty + brokerage + drayage +
    > compliance + inbound; used for pricing and margins.

-   **Accruals** --- Month-end estimates for freight/duty/warehousing
    > not yet invoiced.

-   **Chargebacks / Shortages** --- Carrier or Amazon-assessed
    > adjustments; track and dispute promptly.

-   **A2X** (artifact/system) --- Accounting connector that translates
    > Amazon (and other channels) settlements into structured entries
    > for your ledger.

-   **Non-Amazon Expenses Report** (artifact) --- Monthly roll-up of
    > Shopify/Walmart fees, freight, 3PL invoices,
    > testing/certification, returns handling, marketing-driven
    > logistics.

-   **Duty Drawback / Refunds** --- Recovery mechanism for re-exported
    > goods; specialized but valuable.

# **Reporting Artifacts (your SCM's operating pack)**

-   **PO Tracker** (artifact) --- Line-level view of all POs: dates,
    > supplier, Incoterms, quantities, milestones, exceptions.

-   **Shipment Tracker** (artifact) --- Door-to-door milestones with
    > ETD/ETA, BL/AWB, container IDs, demurrage risk, customs status.

-   **Weekly Stock Report** (artifact) --- FBA/AWD/3PL on-hand, inbound,
    > weeks of cover vs forecast, risk flags; shared with CEO &
    > Marketing.

-   **Overaged Inventory Analysis** (artifact) --- SKUs by aging buckets
    > with fee run-rate and action plan (price cut, promo, removal, AWD
    > diversion).

-   **A2X Reconciliation Pack** (artifact) --- Settlement-to-ledger
    > reconciliation, fee audits, variance notes.

-   **Frequent Returns Analysis** (artifact) --- High-RDR (return defect
    > rate) SKUs, reasons (defect, not as described), corrective actions
    > (QC, packaging, page updates).

-   **Forecast & Buy Plan** (artifact) --- Monthly buy quantities by SKU
    > with FBA/AWD/3PL allocation and ship windows.

-   **Marketing--Supply Chain Alignment Brief** (artifact) --- Monthly
    > plan of promos, ad bursts, forecast lift, and inventory coverage.

# **Compliance, Labeling & Packaging**

-   **GS1 License** --- Ownership of UPC/GTINs; prevents listing
    > conflicts.

-   **FNSKU Label** --- Required unit label for FBA attribution.

-   **Carton & Pallet Labels** --- Amazon-format labels (SSCC-18/Carton
    > ID, Pallet ID); scanned at receiving.

-   **CE/FCC/UL/CSA** --- Compliance marks/testing for electronics, etc.
    > Needed before import/launch.

-   **ISTA Testing** --- Packaging drop/transport tests to reduce damage
    > and returns.

-   **Country of Origin Marking** --- Legal marking rules (e.g., "Made
    > in ...").

# **Systems & Integrations**

-   **ERP** --- Financials, purchasing, inventory valuation (e.g.,
    > NetSuite).

-   **MRP** --- Material planning engine inside ERP.

-   **WMS** --- Warehouse management (3PL or in-house).

-   **TMS** --- Transportation management (rates, tenders, track &
    > trace).

-   **IMS** --- Inventory management layer (sometimes inside ERP/Ecom
    > platform).

-   **EDI / API** --- Electronic data exchange with 3PLs, brokers, and
    > marketplaces.

-   **Vendor Portal** --- Supplier-facing portal for PO visibility and
    > ASNs.

# **Metrics & Service Levels (KPI Canon)**

-   **OTIF (On-Time, In-Full)** --- % of POs that hit
    > ex-factory/delivery dates and quantities.

-   **Lead Time / Lead-Time Adherence** --- Average + variability; the
    > biggest driver of safety stock.

-   **Logistics Cost per Unit** --- End-to-end landed logistics cost
    > divided by sellable units.

-   **Expedite Rate** --- % of units shipped via air/express; proxy for
    > planning quality.

-   **Receiving SLA** --- Days from arrival to available-to-sell at
    > 3PL/AWD/FBA.

-   **RDR (Return Defect Rate)** --- Returns within a period ÷ orders;
    > track per ASIN for Amazon health.

# **Amazon-Specific Workflows**

-   **Inbound Settings (SPD/LTL/FTL)** --- Configures how shipments are
    > routed/created in Seller Central.

-   **Shipment ID / Work ID** --- Identifiers for inbound plans and work
    > content.

-   **Prep Requirements** --- Polybagging, suffocation warnings,
    > bubble-wrap, expiration dating; prevents chargebacks/stranding.

-   **FC Transfer** --- Internal Amazon moves; appear as "reserved";
    > plan for latency.

-   **AWD→FBA Transfers** --- Planned flows from bulk storage to FCs to
    > smooth seasonality.

# **Returns, Customer Feedback & Product Health**

-   **RMA / Return Authorization** --- Mechanism to accept and process
    > returns (non-FBA channels).

-   **Return Reason Codes** --- Categorization (defective, not as
    > described, wrong item); inputs to CAP.

-   **Voice of Customer (VoC)** --- Amazon's product health dashboard;
    > track "Poor" trends to trigger fixes.

# **New Product Introduction (NPI) Artifacts**

-   **Sourcing Dossier** (artifact) --- Quotes, MOQs, lead times,
    > supplier risk notes, compliance needs.

-   **Pilot PO / Pilot Run** --- Limited order to validate quality and
    > demand before scale.

-   **Launch Readiness Checklist** (artifact) --- GS1/UPC, listing
    > assets, compliance certificates, packaging, FBA prep, first-buy
    > plan.

# **Practical "splits" & routing logic (FBA vs AWD vs 3PL)**

-   **FBA Allocation** --- Units sent directly to FCs to maximize
    > availability and Prime promise when sell-through is high.

-   **AWD Allocation** --- Bulk buffer to reduce FBA storage and smooth
    > regional demand; transfer to FCs just-in-time.

-   **3PL Allocation** --- Non-Amazon channels, kitting/prep, seasonal
    > overflows, or when FBA/AWD limits are tight.

-   **Split Rules** (artifact) --- Policy matrix by SKU class: velocity,
    > size tier, seasonality, IPI impact, fee curves → % to FBA/AWD/3PL.

## **Short "How-to" connections between artifacts**

-   **Forecast → Buy Plan → PO Tracker → Shipment Tracker → Weekly Stock
    > Report → Overaged Analysis → A2X Reconciliation\
    > ** That's the core loop tying demand, supply, cash, and fees back
    > into planning.

docs.filename: Supply Chain Manager Resposibilities and routine.docx
docs.content:
# **Responsibilities of Supply Chain Manager**

## **🔹 Daily Responsibilities (Execution & Oversight)**

-   **Operational Control\
    > **

    -   Review active POs, supplier timelines, freight bookings, customs
        > docs, and FBA shipment creation.

    -   Supervise officers: check execution quality, escalate delays,
        > fix errors before they become problems.

-   **Risk & Exceptions\
    > **

    -   Solve urgent shipment delays, incorrect documentation, or
        > prep/label issues for Amazon.

-   **Visibility\
    > **

    -   Update pipeline dashboard (inbound shipments, FBA receiving
        > status, AWD transfers, 3PL movements).

-   **Returns & Quality\
    > **

    -   Monitor daily Amazon return reports for *high-frequency return
        > spikes*. Flag urgent product issues (wrong labeling, defect
        > complaints, mispackaging).

## **🔹 Weekly Responsibilities (Control & Coordination)**

1.  **Inventory & Reporting\
    > **

    -   Prepare **Weekly Stock Report**:

        -   Available Amazon FBA inventory by SKU.

        -   AWD (Amazon Warehousing & Distribution) levels.

        -   3PL stock position.

        -   Stock coverage vs forecasted sales (weeks of cover).

    -   Share stock report with CEO and marketing team to align
        > promotions vs inventory.

2.  **Forecasting & Replenishment\
    > **

    -   Review sales velocity and update **rolling demand forecast**.

    -   Decide replenishment split: **FBA vs AWD vs 3PL** (optimize
        > fees, storage costs, speed to customer).

    -   Identify SKUs needing urgent replenishment.

3.  **Cost & Finance\
    > **

    -   Review **Non-Amazon expenses + A2X reconciliation** (e.g.,
        > Shopify, eBay, freight, duties, 3PL invoices).

    -   Track anomalies in logistics costs, freight overcharges, or
        > warehouse fees.

4.  **Supplier & Logistics\
    > **

    -   Calls with suppliers on production timelines.

    -   Forwarder alignment: ensure enough space booked and freight
        > costs competitive.

    -   3PL checks: receiving, storage, prep SLAs.

5.  **Exception Analysis\
    > **

    -   **Frequent returns analysis (weekly sample)** → check for
        > high-return SKUs, identify if reason is QC, packaging, or
        > product mismatch. Provide corrective actions.

## **🔹 Monthly Responsibilities (Strategy & Performance)**

1.  **Forecasting & Planning\
    > **

    -   Finalize **monthly forecast**:

        -   Based on historical sales, marketing input (planned promos,
            > ads push), and seasonality.

        -   Confirm PO quantities with suppliers.

        -   Allocate flow **(FBA vs AWD vs 3PL)** for the month to
            > balance storage & fulfillment costs.

2.  **Financial Analysis\
    > **

    -   **A2X monthly reconciliation**: confirm Amazon payouts vs COGS,
        > freight, duties, 3PL costs.

    -   **Non-Amazon expenses report** (Shopify, Walmart,
        > marketing-driven logistics, returns handling).

    -   Review **overaged inventory fees analysis** (Amazon long-term
        > fees, AWD surcharges, 3PL storage inefficiencies). Recommend
        > liquidation, removal orders, or AWD diversion.

3.  **Cross-Department Alignment\
    > **

    -   Monthly **connect with Marketing team**:

        -   Align on planned campaigns (Prime Day, seasonal deals, Black
            > Friday).

        -   Ensure supply chain can meet marketing-driven demand without
            > stockouts or excessive storage.

4.  **Strategic Development\
    > **

    -   **New product research support**:

        -   Validate supplier MOQs, lead times, landed cost structure,
            > packaging feasibility for new SKUs.

        -   Provide early supply chain input before product launch.

    -   Review performance of **frequently returned items** → recommend
        > packaging changes, QC improvements, or even product
        > discontinuation.

    -   Supplier diversification & cost renegotiation.

# **📍 Updated SCM Daily Routine (with Additions)**

**08:30 -- 09:30 \| Pipeline & Sales Check**

-   Review Amazon business reports (sales vs forecast).

-   Check FBA/ AWD inventory health, including stranded/aged stock
    > alerts.

-   Update daily dashboard with pipeline + stock risks.

**09:30 -- 11:00 \| Team & Exceptions**

-   Morning sync with Officers: shipments status, delays, PO progress.

-   Escalate urgent issues (delayed container, customs hold, missing
    > ASN).

-   Quick scan of **return reports** for spikes in defects.

**11:00 -- 13:00 \| Supplier & Logistics Calls**

-   Confirm factory production updates.

-   Bookings with forwarders (space secured at reasonable cost).

-   Align with 3PL for incoming shipments.

**13:00 -- 14:00 \| Finance & Reports**

-   Daily/weekly cross-check with A2X & expense tracker.

-   Investigate anomalies in freight invoices or Amazon fee reports.

**14:00 -- 16:00 \| Forecasting & Stock Health**

-   Weekly: Prepare **stock report** (FBA/AWD/3PL).

-   Compare sales velocity vs forecast. Adjust replenishment split (FBA
    > vs AWD vs 3PL).

**16:00 -- 17:30 \| Strategic/Development Tasks**

-   Monthly: Overaged inventory fee analysis.

-   Support marketing team: confirm inventory for promotions.

-   Review **new product feasibility** with suppliers.

-   Analyze high-return SKUs → report to CEO & suggest corrective
    > actions.

# **✅ Final Picture**

### **Daily = execution & firefighting**

-   Shipment tracking, stock health, return alerts.

-   Supervising officers, clearing bottlenecks.

### **Weekly = reporting & tactical control**

-   Stock report (FBA/AWD/3PL).

-   Forecasting update & replenishment allocation.

-   Expense/A2X review, return analysis.

### **Monthly = strategy & optimization**

-   Forecast finalization, order placement & allocation.

-   Overaged fee analysis & financial reports.

-   Marketing team sync & new product planning.

-   Continuous improvement of supply chain efficiency.

👉 With these additions, your SCM is not just an "executor" but a
**business partner** ensuring profitability, scalability, and alignment
between **supply, marketing, and finance**.

docs.filename: Supply Chain Officer Responsibilities and routine.docx
docs.content:
#  **Daily Routine of a Supply Chain Officer**

### **Morning (8:30 -- 10:30 AM) -- Start of Day & Data Checks**

-   **Email & Communication Review**:

    -   Check overnight emails from suppliers, logistics partners, or
        > internal departments (procurement, sales, warehouse).

    -   Flag urgent issues (late shipments, missing documents, inventory
        > discrepancies) for the Supply Chain Manager.

-   **System Updates**:

    -   Log into ERP / WMS (SAP, Oracle, NetSuite, etc.).

    -   Verify inventory levels, new purchase orders, and pending
        > shipments.

    -   Compare system data vs. physical stock reports from the
        > warehouse.

-   **Prepare Daily Dashboard**:

    -   Update spreadsheets or internal dashboards with KPIs (inventory
        > stock-outs, order fulfillment rate, supplier delivery times).

    -   Send a short report to the Manager.

### **Midday (10:30 AM -- 1:00 PM) -- Supplier & Logistics Coordination**

-   **Supplier Follow-ups**:

    -   Contact vendors to confirm order status, shipment schedules, or
        > resolve missing documentation.

    -   Escalate supplier delays to the Manager.

-   **Logistics Tracking**:

    -   Monitor shipments (local couriers, freight forwarders, Amazon
        > FBA inbound).

    -   Check if customs clearance or transport documents are complete.

    -   Update system with expected delivery dates.

-   **Internal Coordination**:

    -   Work with the warehouse team to check inbound/outbound
        > priorities.

    -   Support the sales/e-commerce team with availability info (which
        > SKUs are low or delayed).

### **Afternoon (2:00 -- 4:30 PM) -- Reporting & Support Tasks**

-   **Data Entry & Record Keeping**:

    -   Ensure purchase orders, invoices, and delivery receipts are
        > properly uploaded and filed.

    -   Check for mismatches between supplier invoices and received
        > goods.

-   **Inventory & Replenishment Support**:

    -   Prepare draft reorder suggestions for Manager approval.

    -   Flag slow-moving or high-risk stock.

    -   Update replenishment sheets for Amazon FBA or e-commerce
        > warehouse.

-   **Problem Solving**:

    -   Respond to immediate issues (e.g., stock discrepancies, shipment
        > delays).

    -   Liaise with customer service if supply issues affect order
        > fulfillment.

### **Late Afternoon (4:30 -- 6:00 PM) -- End of Day & Reporting**

-   **Daily Wrap-Up Report**:

    -   Send Manager a short summary:

        -   Supplier delays encountered

        -   Critical inventory levels

        -   Shipments in transit

        -   Issues that need managerial approval/action

-   **Team Support**:

    -   Join quick sync calls with the Manager or warehouse lead.

    -   Take notes for process improvements suggested by the Manager.

-   **Preparation for Tomorrow**:

    -   List pending supplier confirmations.

    -   Flag shipments or inventory checks for follow-up.

# **🎯 Key Takeaways for a Junior Supply Chain Officer**

-   **Routine is highly operational**, not strategic.

-   Main tasks are **data monitoring, supplier/logistics follow-ups, and
    > reporting**.

-   Acts as the "eyes and ears" for the **Supply Chain Manager**.

-   The role is about **execution, accuracy, and communication**,
    > leaving decision-making to the Manager.\
    > \
    > \
    > \
    > \
    > **Weekly Cycle of a Supply Chain Officer (International Dept.)**

## **Daily Responsibilities (Mon--Fri)**

These are done **every day**:

-   **Check emails & comms** (suppliers, forwarders, Amazon
    > notifications).

-   **Update ERP/WMS data**: stock levels, inbound/outbound status.

-   **Monitor shipments in transit**: check forwarder portals, Amazon
    > Shipment Creation Tool, or 3rd-party tracking.

-   **Supplier/forwarder follow-ups**: confirm bookings, pickup dates,
    > or missing docs.

-   **Create small FBA/AWD shipments** (routine replenishments).

-   **Inbound monitoring**: check Amazon FC receiving reports, flag
    > shortages, and prepare to open cases.

-   **Daily report to Supply Chain Manager** with issues (delays,
    > shortages, pending approvals).

## **Weekly Structure**

### **Monday -- Planning & Rate Checks**

-   **Forwarder Rate Comparison**:

    -   Collect weekly rates from 2--3 freight forwarders (air, sea,
        > courier).

    -   Prepare comparison sheet for Manager approval.

-   **Review weekend inbound activity**:

    -   Check Amazon receiving logs for shortages.

    -   Open cases with Amazon for any discrepancies.

    -   Notify suppliers if shortages are supplier-related.

-   **Sync with Manager**: confirm priorities for shipments (SKUs to
    > replenish, suppliers to push).

### **Tuesday -- Supplier & Documentation Focus**

-   **Supplier Confirmations**:

    -   Chase pending POs, confirm ETAs for production readiness.

    -   Request commercial invoices, packing lists, HS codes for
        > customs.

-   **Returns Case Opening**:

    -   For defective or wrong items returned by Amazon/warehouse.

    -   Document evidence (photos, reports) and file claims with
        > suppliers.

-   **Update FBA/AWD Shipment Plans**:

    -   Draft replenishment shipments for Manager review.

### **Wednesday -- Shipments & Logistics**

-   **FBA & AWD Shipments**:

    -   Create bulk replenishment shipments in Seller Central (FBA) or
        > AWD.

    -   Coordinate with forwarders to schedule pickups and book space.

    -   Verify carton labels, pallet labels, export docs.

-   **Transit Monitoring**:

    -   Ensure shipments booked last week have moved (air freight
        > departure, sea freight loaded, courier picked up).

    -   Escalate delays to Manager.

-   **Inventory Review**:

    -   Highlight SKUs at risk of going out of stock in Amazon or 3PL.

### **Thursday -- Problem Solving & Claims**

-   **Amazon Case Management**:

    -   Follow up on previously opened shortage/compensation cases.

    -   Gather PODs, forwarder invoices, and reconcile discrepancies.

-   **Supplier Issues**:

    -   Escalate quality complaints or late production.

    -   Support Manager in negotiation by preparing evidence.

-   **Forwarder Invoice Check**:

    -   Match forwarder charges vs. quotes (catch billing errors).

### **Friday -- Reporting & Wrap-Up**

-   **Weekly Recap Report**:

    -   Prepare a summary for the Manager:

        -   Shipments dispatched this week.

        -   Inbound shipments received & shortages found.

        -   Forwarder rate trends.

        -   Cases opened/closed with Amazon or suppliers.

        -   Inventory at risk for next week.

-   **FBA/AWD Shipment Planning for Next Week**:

    -   Draft shipment plans so the Manager can review early next week.

-   **Process Updates**:

    -   Note recurring problems (e.g., a supplier always short-ships,
        > forwarder delays).

    -   Suggest improvements to Manager.

## **🛠 Key Tools Used in the Week**

-   **Amazon Seller Central** (FBA/AWD shipments, case management).

-   **ERP/WMS/Excel** (inventory tracking, replenishment sheets).

-   **Forwarder Portals / Email Quotes** (rate comparison, shipment
    > status).

-   **Communication Channels**: Email, WhatsApp/WeChat with suppliers,
    > calls with forwarders.

# **🎯 Summary**

The **weekly cycle** for a Supply Chain Officer is about:

-   **Daily monitoring**: shipments, stock, cases.

-   **Weekly rhythm**:

    -   **Mon** → forwarder rates & inbound check.

    -   **Tue** → suppliers & returns cases.

    -   **Wed** → shipment creation & logistics.

    -   **Thu** → case follow-ups & invoice checks.

    -   **Fri** → reporting & planning.

This structure ensures the Manager always has **clear visibility**,
while the Officer handles the operational load of **documentation,
follow-ups, case filing, and reporting**.

**Additional Key Responsibilities**

### **1. Requesting Quotes from Suppliers**

-   **When it happens**:

    -   Usually **before placing a purchase order (PO)**, or when
        > launching a new replenishment cycle.

    -   Can be weekly or ad hoc, depending on sales velocity and stock
        > levels.

-   **Tasks**:

    -   Contact suppliers for updated prices (including MOQ, lead times,
        > incoterms).

    -   Compare supplier offers (sometimes in parallel with forwarder
        > rate checking).

    -   Share findings with the **Supply Chain Manager**, who makes the
        > final order decision.

### **2. Placing Orders with Suppliers**

-   **When it happens**:

    -   After Manager approval of supplier quote and replenishment
        > quantities.

-   **Tasks**:

    -   Draft and send official **Purchase Orders** (POs).

    -   Confirm production timelines and payment terms.

    -   Track supplier acknowledgements and deposit/payment schedules.

### **3. Invoices & Payment System (Orders Spreadsheet)**

-   **When it happens**:

    -   As soon as the supplier issues a **Proforma Invoice (PI)**.

-   **Tasks**:

    -   Enter invoice details (supplier name, PO number, SKU, quantity,
        > unit price, total, payment terms, ETA) into the **Orders
        > Spreadsheet** or ERP.

    -   Ensure alignment with the Manager/Finance team for timely
        > payments.

    -   Keep digital copies of invoices + receipts for future **Amazon
        > claims** (shortages, quality disputes).

    -   Update status column (e.g., *Deposit Paid*, *Balance Pending*,
        > *Paid in Full*).

## **Role Overview**

The Supply Chain Officer is an entry-level position in the International
Department, reporting directly to the Supply Chain Manager. The role is
focused on **operational support**, **execution of daily tasks**, and
**accurate data/document management** across procurement, logistics,
Amazon case management, and quality control.

## **🕒 Daily Responsibilities (Every Day, Mon--Fri)**

-   Check and respond to **emails/messages** (suppliers, forwarders,
    > Amazon).

-   Update **ERP/WMS/Seller Central** with stock and shipment status.

-   Monitor **Amazon inbound receiving** to identify shortages or
    > delays.

-   Support **FBA/AWD shipment creation** when required.

-   Maintain **Orders Spreadsheet** with supplier invoices and payment
    > updates.

-   Provide quick updates to the Manager on urgent issues (delays,
    > shortages, failed QI).

## **📆 Weekly Cycle (Detailed)**

### **Monday -- Planning & Rates**

-   Review weekend inbound activity (Amazon receiving logs).

-   Open Amazon cases for shortages.

-   Notify suppliers if shortages are supplier-related.

-   Collect **weekly freight rates** from forwarders (air, sea,
    > courier).

-   Request **quotes from suppliers** for SKUs flagged as low stock.

-   Compare forwarder rates and supplier quotes → prepare summary for
    > Manager.

### **Tuesday -- Supplier Coordination & Orders**

-   Follow up on **supplier POs & ETAs**.

-   Request updated **commercial invoices, packing lists, HS codes**.

-   With Manager approval: **place POs with suppliers**.

-   Collect and review **Proforma Invoices (PIs)**.

-   Enter invoice details into **Orders Spreadsheet** (amounts, terms,
    > due dates).

-   Open **returns cases** with suppliers or Amazon if needed.

-   Draft **replenishment shipments** for FBA/AWD.

### **Wednesday -- Shipments & Payments**

-   Create and finalize **FBA & AWD shipments** (labels, docs,
    > bookings).

-   Coordinate with forwarders to book pickups (air/sea/courier).

-   Track shipments already in transit (forwarder portals, Seller
    > Central).

-   Enter and update **supplier invoices in Orders Spreadsheet**.

-   Update **payment status** in coordination with Finance/Manager.

-   Conduct **inventory review** and flag SKUs at risk of going out of
    > stock.

### **Thursday -- Claims & Quality Inspections**

-   Check **Amazon case statuses** (shortages, claims, reimbursements).

-   Collect **PODs, invoices, and documents** as claim evidence.

-   Organize **Quality Inspection (QI)**:

    -   Create QI file with product specs, packaging instructions,
        > labeling, AQL levels.

    -   Contact QI company to schedule inspections.

-   Verify **forwarder invoices** against agreed quotes.

-   Confirm **supplier production progress** and payments.

### **Friday -- Reviews & Reporting**

-   Review **QI reports and pictures** received from inspectors.

-   Flag issues to Manager and suppliers, escalate problems.

-   Prepare **weekly recap report** including:

    -   Shipments dispatched

    -   Inbound shortages and Amazon cases

    -   Forwarder rate trends

    -   Supplier PO status and payments

    -   QI results

-   Draft next week's replenishment shipment plans.

-   Ensure **Orders Spreadsheet** is fully up to date.

-   Send weekly summary to Manager.

-   Document recurring problems (supplier delays, forwarder issues,
    > Amazon claims, inspection failures).

-   Suggest improvements to Manager.

## **📦 Quality Inspection (QI) Responsibilities**

-   Create and maintain **QI files** with:

    -   Product specifications

    -   Packaging and labeling requirements

    -   Carton dimensions and weight

    -   AQL levels and inspection methods

    -   Special checks (drop test, barcode scans, polybag suffocation
        > warnings, etc.)

-   Contact QI company to schedule inspections.

-   Review photos, videos, and written reports from inspectors.

-   Report findings to Manager and escalate issues to suppliers if
    > defects or non-compliance found.

## **🛠 Tools & Systems Used**

-   **Amazon Seller Central** → FBA/AWD shipments, inbound monitoring,
    > case management.

-   **Orders Spreadsheet** → POs, invoices, payment tracking.

-   **ERP/WMS** → Inventory and replenishment (if available).

-   **Forwarder portals & email** → Freight rate collection, shipment
    > tracking.

-   **QI file templates** → Product specs, inspection checklist, AQL
    > levels.

-   **Communication apps** → Email, WhatsApp, WeChat
    > (suppliers/forwarders/QI).

## **✅ Summary**

The Supply Chain Officer:

-   Executes **daily monitoring & reporting**.

-   Coordinates with **suppliers, forwarders, and QI companies**.

-   Manages **Amazon cases (shortages, returns, reimbursements)**.

-   Maintains **financial accuracy** via Orders Spreadsheet.

-   Prepares **FBA/AWD shipments** and tracks in-transit cargo.

-   Ensures **quality compliance** through inspections.

-   Provides structured updates and reports to the Supply Chain Manager.

1
PART 1
THE IMPORT BIBLE – BEGINNERS GUIDE
CHAPTER 1
ABOUT THE AUTHOR:
My name is Manuel and I was born in Austria in a little town called
Melk. Asia has always fascinated me, ever since I can remember. I
remember watching Bruce Lee and other Chinese movies as a kid and
I was hooked right away.
My professional career started in 1998 when I worked for Austria's
biggest DIY retailer (bauMax www.baumax.at ). I moved to Hong
Kong in 2005 when I was offered a position as an intern at one of the
biggest sourcing offices in Hong Kong, with a staff over 200 people.
For more than 10 years I worked with the biggest retailers in the
world, developing, sourcing, and finding new products for them.
These retailers included: Metro, Rewe, OBI, Carrefour, Tesco, Wal-
Mart, Amazon, Auchan, Lowes, Sears, Home Depot and many others.
As you can imagine, the big retailers expect their products to do more
than just conform to standards and regulations when they purchase
them in Asia. They need to be of the highest standard and quality.
These retailers cannot afford to have a product recalled due to quality
problems or defective components. These products need to be top-
notch.

2
In those 10 years I got to know all about the standards and product
requirements, and additionally I gained a lot of experience and insight
working with factories in China.
Not just that. I learned a lot about China, its culture, and its
manufacturing facilities: where and how to find the best suppliers,
how to negotiate and communicate with them, how to arrange
business travel for my customers, and a lot more.
In the busiest seasons (April and October), I accompanied up to 30
buyers a month to exhibitions, factories and showrooms to help
negotiate, develop products and follow up on the buyer's requests
after they were gone. I got to know all the tips and tricks you need to
return home from your trip successfully.
This eBook is aimed to make you a professional importer in a few
days, using what took me over 10 years to learn.
This eBook will guide you step-by-step on the process of importing
goods from China.
This eBook aims to take away all your worries and doubts and help
you to take your business to the next level!
I will cover all aspects of importing from China. Starting with several
online supplier sourcing sites such as Globalsources.com and
Alibaba.com, you’ll get to know about importing procedures, dealing
with factories, finding your products, how to inspect and ship your
goods, and finding out what legal import requirements your products
need. In Part 3 I cover taking your business to an even higher level
when going to China, including: travel arrangements, visas, customs
clearance, how to negotiate with suppliers in factories, tips and tricks
to get you the best prices, and simple etiquette in China.

3
I’ve updated this eBook in January 2019 to reflect changes in the
industry as well as insights learned since first publishing this eBook.
Where am I now? I’ve spoken at large conferences around the world
on importing, exporting, Amazon FBA and ecommerce in general. I
now run several online and ecommerce businesses as well as
coaching programs that teach importing from China here:
http://importdojo.com
I also run a personal blog and site over at: www.manuelbecvar.com
On this page I document my entrepreneurial journey as well as
making money online. If you sign up on this site you’ll also receive my
FREE 200+ pages eBook on making money online.
If you have any questions feel free to email me at:
info@manuelbecvar.com
I also invite you to join my FREE Facebook groups where like-minded
entrepreneurs share their ideas, thoughts and process:
Facebook group for making money online and entrepreneurial things:
